sourceQuestion:
Refer to the given 3-address code sequence. This code sequence is split into basic blocks. The number of basic blocks is ________. (Answer in integer)

1001: i = 1
1002: j = 1
1003: t1 = 10*i
1004: t2 = t1+j
1005: t3 = 8*t2
1006: t4 = t3-88
1007: a[t4] = 0.0
1008: j = j+1
1009: if j <= 10 goto 1003
1010: i = i+1
1011: if i <= 10 goto 1002
1012: i = 1
1013: j = 1
1014: t5 = i-1
1015: t6 = 88*t5
1016: t7 = t6 + 8*(j-1)
1017: a[t7] = ...

A. 3
B. 4
C. 5
D. 6

parsed.stem:
Refer to the given 3-address code sequence. This code sequence is split into basic blocks. The number of basic blocks is ________. (Answer in integer)

1001: i = 1
1002: j = 1
1003: t1 = 10*i
1004: t2 = t1+j
1005: t3 = 8*t2
1006: t4 = t3-88
1007: a[t4] = 0.0
1008: j = j+1
1009: if j <= 10 goto 1003
1010: i = i+1
1011: if i <= 10 goto 1002
1012: i = 1
1013: j = 1
1014: t5 = i-1
1015: t6 = 88*t5
1016: t7 = t6 + 8*(j-1)
1017: a[t7] = ...

sourceAnswer: C. S1 is true and S2 is false

options.text: S1 is false and S2 is false
S1 is false and S2 is true
S1 is true and S2 is false
S1 is true and S2 is true

sourceAnswer: D. There is no scope of dead code elimination in this code

options.text: The code contains loop invariant computation
There is scope of common sub-expression elimination in this code
There is scope of strength reduction in this code
There is no scope of dead code elimination in this code

sourceQuestion:
Some code optimizations are carried out on the intermediate code because
A. They enhance the portability of the compiler to the target processor
B. Program analysis is more accurate on intermediate code than on machine code
C. The information from dataflow analysis cannot otherwise be used for optimization
D. The information from the front end cannot otherwise be used for optimization
sourceAnswer: A. They enhance the portability of the compiler to the target processor

parsed.stem: Some code optimizations are carried out on the intermediate code because

options.text: They enhance the portability of the compiler to the target processor
Program analysis is more accurate on intermediate code than on machine code
The information from dataflow analysis cannot otherwise be used for optimization
The information from the front end cannot otherwise be used for optimization

sourceQuestion:
Which one of the following is FALSE?
A. A basic block is a sequence of instructions where control enters the sequence at the beginning and exits at the end.
B. Available expression analysis can be used for common subexpression elimination.
C. Live variable analysis can be used for dead code elimination.
D. z = (4 + 5) => z = 20 is an example of common subexpression elimination.
sourceAnswer: D. z = (4 + 5) => z = 20 is an example of common subexpression elimination.

parsed.stem: Which one of the following is FALSE?

options.text: A basic block is a sequence of instructions where control enters the sequence at the beginning and exits at the end.
Available expression analysis can be used for common subexpression elimination.
Live variable analysis can be used for dead code elimination.
z = (4 + 5) => z = 20 is an example of common subexpression elimination.

sourceQuestion:
Consider the basic block given below.
a = b + c
c = a + d
d = b + c
e = d - b
a = e + b
The minimum number of nodes and edges present in the DAG representation of the above basic block respectively are
A. 6 and 6
B. 8 and 10
C. 9 and 12
D. 8 and 6

a = b + c
c = a + d
d = b + c
e = d - b
a = e + b

sourceQuestion:
In the context of abstract-syntax-tree (AST) and control-flow-graph (CFG), which one of the following is TRUE?
A. In both AST and CFG, let node N_2 be the successor of node N_1. In the input program, the code corresponding to N_2 is present after the code corresponding to N_1.
B. For any input program, neither AST nor CFG will contain a cycle.
C. The maximum number of successors of a node in an AST and a CFG depends on the input program.
D. Each node in both AST and CFG corresponds to at most one statement in the input program.
sourceAnswer: D. Each node in both AST and CFG corresponds to at most one statement in the input program.

parsed.stem: In the context of abstract-syntax-tree (AST) and control-flow-graph (CFG), which one of the following is TRUE?

options.text: In both AST and CFG, let node N_2 be the successor of node N_1. In the input program, the code corresponding to N_2 is present after the code corresponding to N_1.
For any input program, neither AST nor CFG will contain a cycle.
The maximum number of successors of a node in an AST and a CFG depends on the input program.
Each node in both AST and CFG corresponds to at most one statement in the input program.

sourceQuestion:
Which ONE of the following techniques used in compiler code optimization uses live variable analysis?
A. Run-time function call management
B. Register assignment to variables
C. Strength reduction
D. Constant folding

parsed.stem: Which ONE of the following techniques used in compiler code optimization uses live variable analysis?

sourceQuestion:
In a compiler the module that checks every character of the source text is called:
A. The code generator.
B. The code optimiser.
C. The lexical analyser.
D. The syntax analyser.

sourceQuestion:
Match the pairs in the following questions:
(a) Lexical analysis       (p) DAG's
(b) Code optimisation      (q) Syntax trees
(c) Code generation        (r) Push down automaton
(d) Abelian groups         (s) Finite automaton

A. P-4, Q-3, R-1, S-4
B. P-3, Q-4, R-1, S-2
C. P-4, Q-3, R-2, S-1
D. P-2, Q-4, R-1, S-3

(d) Abelian groups         (s) Finite automaton

sourceQuestion:
Consider line number 3 of the following C-program:
int main()
{                      /*Line 1*/
  int I, N;            /*Line 2*/
  fro (I=0, I<N, I++); /*Line 3*/
}
Identify the compiler's response about this line while creating the object-module:
A. No compilation error
B. Only a lexical error
C. Only syntactic errors
D. Both lexical and syntactic errors

sourceQuestion:
Match all items in Group 1 with the correct options from those given in Group 2.

Group 1:
P. Regular Expression
Q. Pushdown automata
R. Dataflow analysis
S. Register allocation

Group 2:
1. Syntax analysis
2. Code generation
3. Lexical analysis
4. Code optimization

A. P-4, Q-1, R-2, S-3
B. P-3, Q-4, R-1, S-2
C. P-4, Q-3, R-2, S-1
D. P-2, Q-4, R-1, S-3

Q. Pushdown automata
R. Dataflow analysis
S. Register allocation

Group 2:
1. Syntax analysis
2. Code generation
3. Lexical analysis
4. Code optimization

sourceQuestion:
Match the following:

P. Lexical analysis       1. Graph coloring
Q. Parsing                2. DFA minimization
R. Register allocation    3. Post-order traversal
S. Expression evaluation  4. Production tree

A. P-2, Q-3, R-1, S-4
B. P-2, Q-4, R-1, S-3
C. P-2, Q-4, R-3, S-1
D. P-2, Q-1, R-4, S-3

sourceQuestion:
Match the following:

(P) Lexical analysis       (i)  Leftmost derivation
(Q) Top-down parsing       (ii) Type checking
(R) Semantic analysis      (iii) Regular expressions
(S) Activation records     (iv)  Runtime administration

A. P -> iii, Q -> i, R -> ii, S -> iv
B. P -> ii, Q -> i, R -> iii, S -> iv
C. P -> iii, Q -> ii, R -> i, S -> iv
D. P -> iii, Q -> i, R -> iv, S -> ii
sourceAnswer: A. P -> iii, Q -> i, R -> ii, S -> iv

parsed.stem:
Match the following:

(P) Lexical analysis       (i)  Leftmost derivation
(Q) Top-down parsing       (ii) Type checking
(R) Semantic analysis      (iii) Regular expressions
(S) Activation records     (iv)  Runtime administration

options.text: P -> iii, Q -> i, R -> ii, S -> iv
P -> ii, Q -> i, R -> iii, S -> iv
P -> iii, Q -> ii, R -> i, S -> iv
P -> iii, Q -> i, R -> iv, S -> ii

sourceQuestion:
Match the following according to input (from the left column) to the compiler phase (in the right column) that processes it:

P. Syntax tree                  i.  Code generator
Q. Character stream             ii. Syntax analyzer
R. Intermediate representation  iii. Semantic analyzer
S. Token stream                 iv. Lexical analyzer

A. P-iii, Q-iv, R-i, S-ii
B. P-iii, Q-i, R-ii, S-iv
C. P-ii, Q-iv, R-iii, S-i
D. P-iv, Q-iii, R-ii, S-i

parsed.stem:
Match the following according to input (from the left column) to the compiler phase (in the right column) that processes it:

P. Syntax tree                  i.  Code generator
Q. Character stream             ii. Syntax analyzer
R. Intermediate representation  iii. Semantic analyzer
S. Token stream                 iv. Lexical analyzer

sourceQuestion:
Which one of the following statements is FALSE?

A. Context-free grammar can be used to specify both lexical and syntax rules
B. Type checking is done before parsing
C. High-level language programs can be translated to different Intermediate Representations
D. Arguments to a function can be passed using the program stack
sourceAnswer: B. Type checking is done before parsing

parsed.stem: Which one of the following statements is FALSE?

options.text: Context-free grammar can be used to specify both lexical and syntax rules
Type checking is done before parsing
High-level language programs can be translated to different Intermediate Representations
Arguments to a function can be passed using the program stack

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following statements.
I. Symbol table is accessed only during lexical analysis and syntax analysis.
II. Compilers for programming languages that support recursion necessarily need heap storage for memory allocation in the run-time environment.
III. Errors violating the condition 'any variable must be declared before its use' are detected during syntax analysis.

Which of the above statements is/are TRUE?
A. I only
B. II and III only
C. II only
D. None of I, II and III
sourceAnswer: D. None of I, II and III

parsed.stem:
Consider the following statements.
I. Symbol table is accessed only during lexical analysis and syntax analysis.
II. Compilers for programming languages that support recursion necessarily need heap storage for memory allocation in the run-time environment.
III. Errors violating the condition 'any variable must be declared before its use' are detected during syntax analysis.

Which of the above statements is/are TRUE?

options.text: None of I, II and III

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following statements regarding the front-end and back-end of a compiler.
S1: The front-end includes phases that are independent of the target hardware.
S2: The back-end includes phases that are specific to the target hardware.
S3: The back-end includes phases that are specific to the programming language used in the source code.

Identify the CORRECT option:
A. Only S1 is TRUE
B. Only S1 and S2 are TRUE
C. S1, S2 and S3 are all TRUE
D. Only S1 and S3 are TRUE
sourceAnswer: B. Only S1 and S2 are TRUE

parsed.stem:
Consider the following statements regarding the front-end and back-end of a compiler.
S1: The front-end includes phases that are independent of the target hardware.
S2: The back-end includes phases that are specific to the target hardware.
S3: The back-end includes phases that are specific to the programming language used in the source code.

Identify the CORRECT option:

options.text: Only S1 and S2 are TRUE
S1, S2 and S3 are all TRUE
Only S1 and S3 are TRUE

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following two sets:
Set X: P. Lexical Analyzer, Q. Syntax Analyzer, R. Intermediate Code Generator, S. Code Optimizer
Set Y: 1. Abstract Syntax Tree, 2. Token, 3. Parse Tree, 4. Constant Folding

Which one of the following options is the CORRECT match from Set X to Set Y?
A. P -> 4, Q -> 1; R -> 3; S -> 2
B. P -> 2, Q -> 3; R -> 1; S -> 4
C. P -> 2, Q -> 1; R -> 3; S -> 4
D. P -> 4, Q -> 2; R -> 1; S -> 3

Which one of the following options is the CORRECT match from Set X to Set Y?

sourceQuestion:
To evaluate an expression without any embedded function calls
A. One stack is enough
B. Two stacks are needed
C. As many stacks as the height of the expression tree are needed
D. A Turing machine is needed in the general case

parsed.stem: To evaluate an expression without any embedded function calls

options.text: As many stacks as the height of the expression tree are needed
A Turing machine is needed in the general case

sourceQuestion:
For a context free grammar, FOLLOW(A) is the set of terminals that can appear immediately to the right of non-terminal A in some "sentential" form. We define two sets LFOLLOW(A) and RFOLLOW(A) by replacing the word "sentential" by "left sentential" and "rightmost sentential" respectively in the definition of FOLLOW(A).

Which of the following statements is/are TRUE?
A. FOLLOW(A) and LFOLLOW(A) are always the same.
B. RFOLLOW(A) and LFOLLOW(A) are always the same.
C. All three sets are identical.
D. All the three sets are different.

parsed.stem:
For a context free grammar, FOLLOW(A) is the set of terminals that can appear immediately to the right of non-terminal A in some "sentential" form. We define two sets LFOLLOW(A) and RFOLLOW(A) by replacing the word "sentential" by "left sentential" and "rightmost sentential" respectively in the definition of FOLLOW(A).

Which of the following statements is/are TRUE?

options.text: FOLLOW(A) and LFOLLOW(A) are always the same.
RFOLLOW(A) and LFOLLOW(A) are always the same.
All the three sets are different.

sourceQuestion:
For the grammar below, a partial LL(1) parsing table is also presented along with the grammar. Entries that need to be filled are indicated as E1 and E2. epsilon is the empty string, $ indicates end of input, and, separates alternate right hand sides of productions.

Grammar:
  S -> aAbB | epsilon
  A -> S
  B -> S

Partial parsing table (rows = non-terminals, columns = a, b, $):
  S: a -> aAbB,  b -> E1,  $ -> E2
  A: a -> S,     b -> S,   $ -> S
  B: a -> S,     b -> S,   $ -> S

What are E1 and E2?
A. E1 = epsilon, E2 = epsilon
B. E1 = epsilon, E2 = aAbB
C. E1 = aAbB,   E2 = epsilon
D. E1 = {epsilon, aAbB}, E2 = epsilon

parsed.stem:
For the grammar below, a partial LL(1) parsing table is also presented along with the grammar. Entries that need to be filled are indicated as E1 and E2. epsilon is the empty string, $ indicates end of input, and, separates alternate right hand sides of productions.

Grammar:
  S -> aAbB | epsilon
  A -> S
  B -> S

Partial parsing table (rows = non-terminals, columns = a, b, $):
  S: a -> aAbB,  b -> E1,  $ -> E2

options.text: a -> S, b -> S, $ -> S What are E1 and E2? A. E1 = epsilon, E2 = epsilon B. E1 = epsilon, E2 = aAbB

parsed.stem:
Consider the following grammar:
P -> QRS
Q -> yz | z
R -> w | epsilon
S -> y
What is FOLLOW(Q)?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following grammar G, with S as the start symbol. The grammar G has three incomplete productions denoted by (1), (2), and (3).

S -> adT  |  (1)
T -> aSb  |  (2)
R -> (3)  |  c

The set of terminals is {a, b, c, d, f}. The FIRST and FOLLOW sets of the different non-terminals are as follows.

FIRST(S) = {a, d, f}, FIRST(T) = {a, b, c}, FIRST(R) = {c, e*}  [where e* denotes epsilon]
FOLLOW(S) = FOLLOW(T) = {c, f}, FOLLOW(R) = {f}

Which one of the following options CORRECTLY fills in the incomplete productions?

A. (1) S -> fR(2) T -> eR(3) R -> cR
B. (1) S -> fR(2) T -> eR(3) R -> eR
C. (1) S -> fR(2) T -> cR(3) R -> eR
D. (1) S -> fR(2) T -> eR(3) R -> eR -> cR

parsed.stem:
Consider the following grammar G, with S as the start symbol. The grammar G has three incomplete productions denoted by (1), (2), and (3).

S -> adT  |  (1)
T -> aSb  |  (2)
R -> (3)  |  c

The set of terminals is {a, b, c, d, f}. The FIRST and FOLLOW sets of the different non-terminals are as follows.

FIRST(S) = {a, d, f}, FIRST(T) = {a, b, c}, FIRST(R) = {c, e*}  [where e* denotes epsilon]
FOLLOW(S) = FOLLOW(T) = {c, f}, FOLLOW(R) = {f}

Which one of the following options CORRECTLY fills in the incomplete productions?

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following statement(s) is/are TRUE while computing FIRST and FOLLOW during top down parsing by a compiler?

A. For a production A -> epsilon, epsilon will be added to FIRST(A).
B. If there is any input right end marker $, it will be added to FOLLOW(S), where S is the start symbol.
C. For a production A -> alpha B beta, FIRST(beta) \ {epsilon} will be added to FOLLOW(B).
D. If there is any production A -> alpha B, epsilon in FOLLOW(A) will be added to FOLLOW(B), where S is the start symbol.

parsed.stem: Which of the following statement(s) is/are TRUE while computing FIRST and FOLLOW during top down parsing by a compiler?

options.text: For a production A -> epsilon, epsilon will be added to FIRST(A).
If there is any input right end marker $, it will be added to FOLLOW(S), where S is the start symbol.
For a production A -> alpha B beta, FIRST(beta) \ {epsilon} will be added to FOLLOW(B).
If there is any production A -> alpha B, epsilon in FOLLOW(A) will be added to FOLLOW(B), where S is the start symbol.

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following grammar G for arithmetic expressions using binary operators - and / which are not associative:

E -> E - T | T
T -> T / F | F
F -> (E) | id

(E is the start symbol)

Which of the following is generated by this grammar?
A. id - id - id
B. id / id / id
C. id - id / id
D. id / id - id

parsed.stem:
Consider the following grammar G for arithmetic expressions using binary operators - and / which are not associative:

E -> E - T | T
T -> T / F | F
F -> (E) | id

(E is the start symbol)

Which of the following is generated by this grammar?

sourceQuestion:
If G is a context free grammar and w is a string of length l in L(G), how long is a derivation of w in G, if G is in Chomsky Normal Form?

A. 2l
B. 2l + 1
C. 2l - 1
D. l

parsed.stem: If G is a context free grammar and w is a string of length l in L(G), how long is a derivation of w in G, if G is in Chomsky Normal Form?

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following features cannot be captured by context-free grammars?

A. Syntax of if-then-else statements
B. Syntax of recursive procedures
C. Whether a variable has been declared before its use
D. Variable names of arbitrary length
sourceAnswer: C. Whether a variable has been declared before its use

parsed.stem: Which of the following features cannot be captured by context-free grammars?

options.text: Whether a variable has been declared before its use
